title: Renal Cell Carcinoma
oneLiner: Most common renal malignancy (peak age 60–70), often incidental; classic triad (haematuria, flank pain, mass) now rare — think of it in unexplained haematuria.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** sudden unilateral flank/abdominal pain + hypotension — bedside: pulsatile mass, soft abdomen → urgent CT abdomen (if unstable, call vascular surg. immediately)
- **Septic shock from obstructive pyonephrosis:** loin pain + fever + rigors — bedside: raised WCC, CRP, hypotension; urgent renal USS — visible hydronephrosis → emergency drainage (nephrostomy or ureteric stent)
- **Hypercalcaemic crisis (paraneoplastic):** fluctuating consciousness, polyuria, dehydration — check corrected calcium >3.5 mmol/L with ECG (short QT) — give IV fluids, consider bisphosphonate but [check local policy] for dose — do not wait for senior.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible haematuria (painless or with flank pain)
  - Incidental finding on imaging (US, CT, MRI for other reasons)
  - Flank pain (dull ache or acute clot colic)
  - Unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fevers (paraneoplastic)
  - Symptoms of metastases: bone pain, cough, hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable abdominal mass (with ballotability)
  - Left-sided varicocele (especially acute, not emptying on lying down — indicates left renal vein involvement)
  - Hypertension (renin secretion or vascular compression)
  - Anaemia (or polycythaemia) — check Hb
  - Signs of venous thromboembolism (swollen leg, chest pain)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Renal cyst (Bosniak III/IV):** simple cyst — no enhancement on CT; complex cyst — internal septations, walls, but still less solid than RCC. **Splitter:** contrast-enhanced CT: solid enhancement = RCC; no enhancement = cyst.
- **Angiomyolipoma (AML):** contains fat — visible on CT (negative Hounsfield units), often in women of childbearing age; can bleed spontaneously.
- **Oncocytoma:** benign tumour; looks like RCC but often central scar; cannot differentiate pre-operatively without biopsy.
- **Transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of renal pelvis:** haematuria + flank pain (ureteral involvement), but no mass on exam; key: filling defect on CT urogram or ureteroscopy.
- **Renal infarct:** acute flank pain + haematuria with atrial fibrillation — no mass; CT shows wedge-shaped non-enhancing area.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (blood) + urine culture (exclude infection)
  - NEWS2 + blood pressure (hypertension common)
  - ECG if hypercalcaemia suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia/polycythaemia)
  - U+E + creatinine (baseline renal function)
  - Calcium (corrected) — always check in any malignancy
  - LDH, albumin, liver profile (metastatic load)
  - Coagulation screen (before biopsy/surgery)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (triple-phase: non-contrast, nephrographic, excretory) — gold standard for diagnosis and staging (local + renal vein involvement)
  - **Second-line:** Renal US (if contrast contraindicated: pregnancy, allergy, eGFR <30) — can show mass, complex cysts, hydronephrosis
  - **Third-line:** MRI (equivocal cases, contraindication to CT contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Biopsy: only if diagnosis is unclear (e.g., suspected lymphoma, abscess) or before ablative therapy — usually not required before nephrectomy
  - Chest CT (staging for pulmonary metastases)
  - Bone scan or PET-CT if bone pain or high-risk disease
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CT abdomen with contrast** — omission delays diagnosis and risks progression; if CT contraindicated, urgent renal US + referral to urology for MRI.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemorrhage (spontaneous rupture or bleed into mass): IV fluid resuscitation, cross-match, urgent CT, call urology and interventional radiology for possible embolisation
  - If hypercalcaemia crisis: IV 0.9% saline 2–3 litres over 4–6 hours (monitor urine output); [check local policy] for bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid or pamidronate)
  - If sepsis from obstruction: IV antibiotics, renal USS, and emergency nephrostomy/stenting
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Staging: CT chest, bone scan if symptomatic
  - MDT discussion (urology, oncology, radiology)
  - Smoking cessation advice
  - Active surveillance (small renal masses <4 cm in elderly/comorbid) vs partial nephrectomy (preserve renal function) vs radical nephrectomy vs ablative therapies (cryotherapy, radiofrequency)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up imaging (CT at 3–6 months then annually)
  - Manage hypertension (ACEi or ARB first line)
  - Consider targeted therapy (sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib) or immunotherapy (nivolumab + ipilimumab) for metastatic disease — referral to oncology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** maintenance IVI (1 L 0.9% saline over 8 hours) if oral intake inadequate; if hypercalcaemia, aggressive IV fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk or active haemorrhage)
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for intervention
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 + BP
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (for metastases):** sunitinib 50 mg PO daily for 4 weeks on/2 weeks off, or pazopanib 800 mg PO daily — specialist oncology prescription; [check local policy] for exact dosing
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cabozantinib 60 mg PO daily
  - Nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks (check local protocol)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs): avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges (CYP3A4 interaction); caution in hypertension, hypothyroidism (monitor)
  - Immunotherapy (nivolumab): risk of immune-related adverse events (colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis) — educate patient to report diarrhoea, rash, dyspnoea
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - For TKIs: reduce dose if moderate hepatic impairment; no dose adjustment for mild renal impairment
  - Bisphosphonates: avoid if eGFR <30–35 (except after careful consideration and dose reduction); monitor calcium, phosphate, magnesium
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid):** failure to check renal function before infusion — risk of acute renal failure; give only if eGFR ≥35 and correct hypocalcaemia first
  - **Sunitinib:** starting at full dose in severe hypertension (BP not controlled) — risk of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) — check BP before each cycle
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Spontaneous tumour rupture with haemorrhage (flank pain + shock)
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) — tumour prothrombotic state
  - Hypercalcaemia (paraneoplastic), hypertension crisis
  - Sepsis from obstructive pyonephrosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Renal impairment after nephrectomy (especially if single kidney or pre-existing CKD)
  - Recurrence (local or metastatic) — even after curative resection
  - Side effects of targeted therapy: hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhoea, fatigue, hypothyroidism, hypertension, hepatotoxicity
sections.Prognosis:
- Localised (stage I–II): 5-year survival >90% (if surgically resected)
- Locally advanced (stage III): 5-year survival 50–70%
- Metastatic (stage IV): 5-year survival <20% (but now improving with targeted therapy/immunotherapy — median survival ~2–3 years)
- Early detection (incidental on imaging) improves outcome significantly
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always order CT abdomen with contrast** for any unexplained visible haematuria in patients >40 (even if US normal)
- **Check serum calcium in every patient with new haematuria + weight loss** — hypercalcaemia can be the first clue
- **Examine for left-sided varicocele** in any man with flank pain — if non-reducing, suspect renal vein thrombosis from RCC
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not dismiss visible haematuria as UTI** without imaging — send urine culture but always follow up with CT if culture negative or if haematuria persists
- **Do not assume a renal mass is a simple cyst without characterising on CT** — Bosniak classification is needed; simple cysts (Bosniak I) are benign, but complex cysts (III/IV) may require surgery
- **Do not give high-dose steroids for suspected renal colic** if there is a possibility of RCC with paraneoplastic syndrome — steroids can worsen hypercalcaemia
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Visible haematuria recurs or becomes heavy (with clots, passing clots, unable to void)
  - Severe flank pain not controlled by simple analgesia
  - Unexplained fever, chills, or loin tenderness (possible pyonephrosis)
  - Signs of DVT (swollen calf, leg pain) or PE (chest pain, dyspnoea, haemoptysis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urology clinic within 2 weeks of discharge
  - If mass found, CT staging should be completed before first appointment
  - Make appointment for smoking cessation clinic if current smoker
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L
  - Acute hypotension + flank pain (suggesting rupture or AAA)
  - Rapidly dropping Hb (likely haemorrhage)
  - Oliguria / anuria (obstruction)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13
  - Septic shock not responding to fluid challenge (MAP <65 after 30 mL/kg)
  - Hypercalcaemia with cardiac arrhythmia or seizures
  - Active haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion

rawMarkdown:
## Renal Cell Carcinoma
**One-liner:** Most common renal malignancy (peak age 60–70), often incidental; classic triad (haematuria, flank pain, mass) now rare — think of it in unexplained haematuria.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** sudden unilateral flank/abdominal pain + hypotension — bedside: pulsatile mass, soft abdomen → urgent CT abdomen (if unstable, call vascular surg. immediately)
- **Septic shock from obstructive pyonephrosis:** loin pain + fever + rigors — bedside: raised WCC, CRP, hypotension; urgent renal USS — visible hydronephrosis → emergency drainage (nephrostomy or ureteric stent)
- **Hypercalcaemic crisis (paraneoplastic):** fluctuating consciousness, polyuria, dehydration — check corrected calcium >3.5 mmol/L with ECG (short QT) — give IV fluids, consider bisphosphonate but [check local policy] for dose — do not wait for senior.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible haematuria (painless or with flank pain)
  - Incidental finding on imaging (US, CT, MRI for other reasons)
  - Flank pain (dull ache or acute clot colic)
  - Unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fevers (paraneoplastic)
  - Symptoms of metastases: bone pain, cough, hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable abdominal mass (with ballotability)
  - Left-sided varicocele (especially acute, not emptying on lying down — indicates left renal vein involvement)
  - Hypertension (renin secretion or vascular compression)
  - Anaemia (or polycythaemia) — check Hb
  - Signs of venous thromboembolism (swollen leg, chest pain)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Renal cyst (Bosniak III/IV):** simple cyst — no enhancement on CT; complex cyst — internal septations, walls, but still less solid than RCC. **Splitter:** contrast-enhanced CT: solid enhancement = RCC; no enhancement = cyst.
- **Angiomyolipoma (AML):** contains fat — visible on CT (negative Hounsfield units), often in women of childbearing age; can bleed spontaneously.
- **Oncocytoma:** benign tumour; looks like RCC but often central scar; cannot differentiate pre-operatively without biopsy.
- **Transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of renal pelvis:** haematuria + flank pain (ureteral involvement), but no mass on exam; key: filling defect on CT urogram or ureteroscopy.
- **Renal infarct:** acute flank pain + haematuria with atrial fibrillation — no mass; CT shows wedge-shaped non-enhancing area.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (blood) + urine culture (exclude infection)
  - NEWS2 + blood pressure (hypertension common)
  - ECG if hypercalcaemia suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia/polycythaemia)
  - U+E + creatinine (baseline renal function)
  - Calcium (corrected) — always check in any malignancy
  - LDH, albumin, liver profile (metastatic load)
  - Coagulation screen (before biopsy/surgery)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (triple-phase: non-contrast, nephrographic, excretory) — gold standard for diagnosis and staging (local + renal vein involvement)
  - **Second-line:** Renal US (if contrast contraindicated: pregnancy, allergy, eGFR <30) — can show mass, complex cysts, hydronephrosis
  - **Third-line:** MRI (equivocal cases, contraindication to CT contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Biopsy: only if diagnosis is unclear (e.g., suspected lymphoma, abscess) or before ablative therapy — usually not required before nephrectomy
  - Chest CT (staging for pulmonary metastases)
  - Bone scan or PET-CT if bone pain or high-risk disease
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CT abdomen with contrast** — omission delays diagnosis and risks progression; if CT contraindicated, urgent renal US + referral to urology for MRI.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemorrhage (spontaneous rupture or bleed into mass): IV fluid resuscitation, cross-match, urgent CT, call urology and interventional radiology for possible embolisation
  - If hypercalcaemia crisis: IV 0.9% saline 2–3 litres over 4–6 hours (monitor urine output); [check local policy] for bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid or pamidronate)
  - If sepsis from obstruction: IV antibiotics, renal USS, and emergency nephrostomy/stenting
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Staging: CT chest, bone scan if symptomatic
  - MDT discussion (urology, oncology, radiology)
  - Smoking cessation advice
  - Active surveillance (small renal masses <4 cm in elderly/comorbid) vs partial nephrectomy (preserve renal function) vs radical nephrectomy vs ablative therapies (cryotherapy, radiofrequency)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up imaging (CT at 3–6 months then annually)
  - Manage hypertension (ACEi or ARB first line)
  - Consider targeted therapy (sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib) or immunotherapy (nivolumab + ipilimumab) for metastatic disease — referral to oncology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** maintenance IVI (1 L 0.9% saline over 8 hours) if oral intake inadequate; if hypercalcaemia, aggressive IV fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk or active haemorrhage)
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for intervention
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 + BP

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (for metastases):** sunitinib 50 mg PO daily for 4 weeks on/2 weeks off, or pazopanib 800 mg PO daily — specialist oncology prescription; [check local policy] for exact dosing
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cabozantinib 60 mg PO daily
  - Nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks (check local protocol)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs): avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges (CYP3A4 interaction); caution in hypertension, hypothyroidism (monitor)
  - Immunotherapy (nivolumab): risk of immune-related adverse events (colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis) — educate patient to report diarrhoea, rash, dyspnoea
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - For TKIs: reduce dose if moderate hepatic impairment; no dose adjustment for mild renal impairment
  - Bisphosphonates: avoid if eGFR <30–35 (except after careful consideration and dose reduction); monitor calcium, phosphate, magnesium
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid):** failure to check renal function before infusion — risk of acute renal failure; give only if eGFR ≥35 and correct hypocalcaemia first
  - **Sunitinib:** starting at full dose in severe hypertension (BP not controlled) — risk of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) — check BP before each cycle

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Spontaneous tumour rupture with haemorrhage (flank pain + shock)
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) — tumour prothrombotic state
  - Hypercalcaemia (paraneoplastic), hypertension crisis
  - Sepsis from obstructive pyonephrosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Renal impairment after nephrectomy (especially if single kidney or pre-existing CKD)
  - Recurrence (local or metastatic) — even after curative resection
  - Side effects of targeted therapy: hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhoea, fatigue, hypothyroidism, hypertension, hepatotoxicity

### Prognosis
- Localised (stage I–II): 5-year survival >90% (if surgically resected)
- Locally advanced (stage III): 5-year survival 50–70%
- Metastatic (stage IV): 5-year survival <20% (but now improving with targeted therapy/immunotherapy — median survival ~2–3 years)
- Early detection (incidental on imaging) improves outcome significantly

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always order CT abdomen with contrast** for any unexplained visible haematuria in patients >40 (even if US normal)
- **Check serum calcium in every patient with new haematuria + weight loss** — hypercalcaemia can be the first clue
- **Examine for left-sided varicocele** in any man with flank pain — if non-reducing, suspect renal vein thrombosis from RCC

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not dismiss visible haematuria as UTI** without imaging — send urine culture but always follow up with CT if culture negative or if haematuria persists
- **Do not assume a renal mass is a simple cyst without characterising on CT** — Bosniak classification is needed; simple cysts (Bosniak I) are benign, but complex cysts (III/IV) may require surgery
- **Do not give high-dose steroids for suspected renal colic** if there is a possibility of RCC with paraneoplastic syndrome — steroids can worsen hypercalcaemia

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Visible haematuria recurs or becomes heavy (with clots, passing clots, unable to void)
  - Severe flank pain not controlled by simple analgesia
  - Unexplained fever, chills, or loin tenderness (possible pyonephrosis)
  - Signs of DVT (swollen calf, leg pain) or PE (chest pain, dyspnoea, haemoptysis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urology clinic within 2 weeks of discharge
  - If mass found, CT staging should be completed before first appointment
  - Make appointment for smoking cessation clinic if current smoker

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L
  - Acute hypotension + flank pain (suggesting rupture or AAA)
  - Rapidly dropping Hb (likely haemorrhage)
  - Oliguria / anuria (obstruction)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13
  - Septic shock not responding to fluid challenge (MAP <65 after 30 mL/kg)
  - Hypercalcaemia with cardiac arrhythmia or seizures
  - Active haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion
